---
title: "Module 9 R Programming"
author: "Vashay Carpenter"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---
Install packages
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
#install.packages("pdftools") 
#install.packages("stringr") required to use str_split
#install.packages("ggplot2")
#install.packages("tidyverse")
#install.packages("corrgram")
library(stringr)
library(pdftools)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
```

## R Markdown
We will first create a usable csv file for our plot

```{r cars}
# Load the CSV data; disable automatic row naming to preserve structure
df_raw <- read.csv("~/r-programming-assignments/STL_Enforcement_Master.csv", row.names = NULL)

colnames(df_raw) <- c("Metric", "Total", "White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Native", "Asian", "Other")

# 1. Filter for a wider range of variables
# Added 'Resist Arrest', 'Weapon', and 'Drug Violation'
metrics <- df_raw[df_raw$Metric %in% c("Stop rate", "Search rate", "Resist Arrest", "Weapon", "Drug Violation", "Contraband hit rate"), ]

# 2. Flip it (Transposing)
df_plot <- as.data.frame(t(metrics[,-1]))
colnames(df_plot) <- metrics$Metric

# 3. Clean and Save (Do this BEFORE the plot)
df_plot <- df_plot %>% 
    mutate(across(everything(), ~as.numeric(as.character(.))))
df_plot$Race <- rownames(df_plot)

#Focus on demographic disparity
df_plot <- df_plot %>% filter(Race != "Total")

# Save the multi-variable data for the assignment/blog
write.csv(df_plot, "Flipped_Enforcement_Data_RProgramming.csv", row.names = FALSE)

```
Plotting the data used to create our new csv file
```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = `Search rate`, y = `Contraband hit rate`, color = Race, size = `Stop rate`)) +
  #Set point transparency to 0.8 to manage overlap (Balance)
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  #Place race labels near points for immediate identification (Proximity)
  geom_text(aes(label = Race), vjust = -1.5, fontface = "bold", size = 4, show.legend = FALSE) + 
  #Ensure the Y-axis has breathing room for labels (Balance)
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.2)))+
  #Use Set1 palette for maximum color differentiation (Contrast)
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + 
  #Clean title; subtitle removed for simplicity (Simplicity/Alignment)  
  labs(title = "Missouri Enforcement Paradox: Contraband Found",
       x = "Search Rate (%)", y = "Contraband Hit Rate (%)") +
  #Use a minimal theme to remove visual clutter (Balance)
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = `Search rate`, y = `Resist Arrest`, color = Race, size = `Stop rate`)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Race), vjust = -1.5, fontface = "bold") + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.2)))+
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + 
  labs(title = "Missouri Enforcement Paradox: Resisting Arrest",
       x = "Search Rate (%)", y = "Resisting Arrest Rate (count)") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = `Search rate`, y = `Weapon`, color = Race, size = `Stop rate`)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Race), vjust = -1.5, fontface = "bold", size = 4, show.legend = FALSE) + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.2)))+
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + 
  labs(title = "Missouri Enforcement Paradox: Illegal Weapons",
       x = "Search Rate (%)", y = "Weapon Rate (count)") +
  theme_minimal()

```

https://rstue.blogspot.com/2026/03/module-9-multivariate-visualizations.html
